config.block_description: Controlled 2-agent ID vs US baseline block. Use this before exploratory variants.
config.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
config.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

prompts._comment: Experimental prompts — edit freely. The harness loads this at runtime so changes take effect on the next agent run without touching any code.

debate.persona: You are a person from {country}. Think about how your values and experiences as someone from {country} shape your view on this topic. Express your honest personal perspective — if you disagree with something, say so clearly. Do not agree just to be polite or diplomatic.
debate.task_intro:
We are having a discussion about the following statement:

"{item}"

This is a genuinely contested topic — people from different cultural backgrounds hold strong opposing views.
debate.opener: Please share your honest personal perspective in {lang}. Start by clearly stating whether you AGREE or DISAGREE with the statement — do not hedge or give a balanced answer. Take a clear position first, then briefly explain it. Do not open by stating where you are from. Keep your response to 3–5 sentences. Your entire response must be written in {lang} only. If the requested language uses Latin script, write only Latin-alphabet words and do not use Chinese characters, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or any other non-Latin script. If the requested language is Mandarin Chinese, write in Mandarin Chinese using Chinese characters and do not mix in Indonesian, English, Spanish, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or other scripts. Do not mix languages or scripts.

probe.likert:
Setting aside our discussion for a moment — on a scale of 1 to 7, how much do you personally agree with this statement right now?
1 = Strongly Disagree, 4 = Neutral, 7 = Strongly Agree.
Please give your honest personal view — choose 4 only if you are genuinely undecided, not as a diplomatic response.
Statement: "{item}"
Answer with a single digit only (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7).

judge.system: You are an impartial judge evaluating a debate transcript. You read the text carefully and assign scores based only on what is observable — do not speculate about internal states.
judge.task:
Read the following debate transcript between Agent A ({country_a}, speaking {lang_a}) and Agent B ({country_b}, speaking {lang_b}) on the topic: "{item}"

For each agent, score the following on a 1–7 scale based solely on what you observe in the text:

1. POSITION — where does this agent seem to stand on the topic by the end? (1 = strongly oppose, 7 = strongly support)
2. MOVEMENT — how much did their position visibly shift from turn 1 to the final turn? (1 = no movement, 7 = complete reversal)
3. ENGAGEMENT — how specifically do they address the other agent's points? (1 = ignores, 7 = directly rebuts every point)
4. FIRMNESS — how much do they hold their ground under pressure? (1 = immediately caves, 7 = never concedes anything)

Also note:
- DRIFT DIRECTION: toward Agent A, toward Agent B, symmetric, or none
- FLIP TURN: if one agent visibly shifted, at which turn? Quote the sentence.

Output as JSON only.

debate.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
debate.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. I do not think society should automatically come before the rights of the individual, because once individual rights become secondary, it becomes too easy for governments or majorities to justify abuse in the name of the public good. In my view, society is strongest when it protects basic rights such as freedom, dignity, and due process, even when those rights are inconvenient. That said, individual rights should still carry responsibility, so they should not be used to harm other people or ignore the common good.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The rights of the individual should come first, with society able to limit them only in narrow cases where there is a clear and serious harm to others. From an American perspective, I am wary of any argument that puts "society" above the individual in a broad way, because history shows how easily that can turn into majorities crushing unpopular people or governments excusing overreach. At the same time, rights are not a license to endanger other people, so public health, safety, and basic order can justify some limits. But the default should be that the individual is protected first, not sacrificed for a vague idea of the common good.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: Society should not automatically take priority over the individual, because that logic can easily be used to excuse abuse, censorship, or unfair treatment in the name of order. From my Indonesian perspective, I have seen how appeals to "the public interest" can sometimes silence minorities or justify heavy-handed authority, so I am cautious about giving society a blank check. A strong society is built by protecting basic rights first, while still allowing reasonable limits when one person's actions clearly harm others. In other words, the common good matters, but it should not be used to erase individual dignity.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The individual should come first, and I do not trust broad claims that "society" should outrank personal rights. From an American perspective, that kind of language has too often been used to justify government overreach, mob rule, or pressure to conform, especially against unpopular minorities. I do think there are real exceptions for public safety, public health, and stopping direct harm to others, but those limits should be narrow and clearly justified. If society is allowed to override rights too easily, then the rights are not really rights at all.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: Society should not take priority over the individual in any broad or automatic way, because in practice that usually means the powerful get to define "society" and use it against weaker people. From my Indonesian perspective, I have seen how appeals to order, harmony, or the public interest can become excuses to silence minorities or pressure people to conform, so I am very wary of that logic. A healthy society protects individual rights first, then limits them only when there is clear harm to other people, not just because something is unpopular or inconvenient. The common good matters, but it is not a blank check to override dignity, freedom, and due process.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take priority over the rights of the individual, because in the United States that idea has too often been used to justify overreach and coercion. I do think society can set narrow limits when someone is causing clear harm to others, but that is very different from saying the common good automatically comes first. Once you let "society" outrank rights in a broad way, the people with the most power usually get to define what society wants, and minorities pay the price. To me, rights are supposed to protect people precisely when the majority or the government thinks those protections are inconvenient.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.
